steps.title: Spot the sarcastic signal
steps.content: The passage opens with Day's striking appearance—features suited for a bust or stamp, an evidently brilliant forehead—but then uses sarcasm to undermine it. The narrator says it would be “impertinent to inquire too closely” into the actual achievements of his fifty-year career, implying those accomplishments are either absent or negligible enough that questioning them would be socially awkward.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the answer
steps.content: Choice A captures the contrast the sarcasm reveals: Day has the appearance of distinction, but his real accomplishments remain in doubt—a gap the memoirs cannot close because they bury substantive details until page thirty. The other choices either invent details the passage rejects or contradict what it clearly establishes about his appearance. That matches choice A — A is correct.
